question: What is the grace period allowed for a policyholder who has chosen a monthly premium frequency?
ground_truth: A grace period of 15 days from the premium due date is allowed for monthly frequency.
vectorRAG_answer:
The grace period allowed for a policyholder who has chosen a monthly premium frequency is 15 days for payment of due instalment premium. 

This information can be found in [Passage 1, Page 2] and [Passage 2, Page 2]. Both passages state: "If you are unable to pay Instalment Premium by the due date, you will be given a grace period of 15 days for payment of due instalment premium if You have chosen monthly frequency..."

premium if You have chosen monthly frequency, and 30 days for
payment of due instalment premium if You have chosen any other
frequency commending form the premium due date. The life cover
continues during the grace period. In case of Death of Life Assured
during the grace period, We will pay the Death Benefit as per the
terms and conditions of the Policy.
6.Renewal Premium in Advance
Collection of renewal premium in advance shall be allowed within the
same  financial  year  for  the  premium  due  in  that  financial  year.
However, where the renewal premium due in one financial year is
being collected in advance in earlier financial year, we may collect the
same for a maximum period of three months in advance from the due
date of the premium. The renewal premium so collected in advance
shall only be adjusted on the due date of the premium.

question: After how many years from the date of policy issuance, risk commencement, revival, or rider addition can a life insurance policy not be questioned on any ground whatsoever?
ground_truth: No policy of Life Insurance shall be called in question on any ground whatsoever after the expiry of 3 years from these dates, according to Section 45.
vectorRAG_answer:
A life insurance policy cannot be questioned on any ground whatsoever after expiry of 3 years from the date of issuance of policy or the date of commencement of risk or the date of revival of policy or the date of rider to the policy, whichever is later [Page 6]. 

This information is explicitly stated in the context, specifically in Passage 1 and Passage 3, which mention the provisions regarding policy not being called into question in terms of Section 45 of the Insurance Act, 1938, as amended by Insurance Laws (Amendment) Act, 2015.
graphRAG_answer:

The relevant triplet is:
[1] (Policy of Life Insurance [FINANCIAL INSTRUMENT]) —[Cannot Be Called Into Question After] {source_page=6, source_chunk=66, confidence=0.95}→ (3 years [TIME PERIOD])

This triplet indicates that a Policy of Life Insurance cannot be called into question after 3 years.

Therefore, the answer is: 
3 years

I used the entity "Policy of Life Insurance" and the relationship "Cannot Be Called Into Question After" to form this answer, which is referenced in triplet [1].

question: What are the three authorized frequencies for premium payments under this policy?
ground_truth: The premiums can be paid in yearly, half-yearly, or monthly frequencies.
vectorRAG_answer:
The three authorized frequencies for premium payments under this policy are:

1. Half-yearly [Page 2]
2. Monthly [Page 2]
3. Yearly [Page 2]

These frequencies are mentioned in the context as having associated loadings, with half-yearly and monthly payments incurring loadings of 2.5% and 4.5% of the premium, respectively, while yearly payments incur no loading [Page 2].
graphRAG_answer:
The three authorized frequencies for premium payments under this policy are Yearly, half-yearly, and monthly. 

This information is based on triplet [1] (Premiums [PAYMENT]) —[Can Be Paid In]→ (Yearly, half-yearly or monthly frequency [PAYMENT FREQUENCY]), which indicates that premiums can be paid in these frequencies. Additionally, triplet [2] confirms this relationship in the reverse direction, further supporting the authorized frequencies for premium payments.

2.Maturity Benefit:
i. On survival of the Life Assured till the Date of Maturity, for a fully
paid policy, the following will be payable: Maturity Benefit = Higher of
(D,  E) Where,  D  =  Guaranteed  Maturity  Benefit  (GMB)  +  accrued
Guaranteed Additions  + subsisting reversionary bonuses accrued, if
declared  + terminal bonus, if declared E = 100.1% X (annualised
premium) ii. The Guaranteed Maturity Benefit (GMB) will be set at
policy  inception  and  is  as  mentioned  in  the  policy  schedule. iii.
Maturity benefit may be taxable as per the prevailing tax laws. iv. On
payment of Maturity Benefit to You, the policy will terminate and all
rights, benefits and interests under the policy will stand extinguished.

continues during the grace period. In case of Death of Life Assured
during the grace period, We will pay the Death Benefit as per the
terms and conditions of the Policy.
6.Renewal Premium in Advance
Collection of renewal premium in advance shall be allowed within the
same  financial  year  for  the  premium  due  in  that  financial  year.
However, where the renewal premium due in one financial year is
being collected in advance in earlier financial year, we may collect the
same for a maximum period of three months in advance from the due
date of the premium. The renewal premium so collected in advance
shall only be adjusted on the due date of the premium.

1.Free look Period (30 days refund policy)
On receipt of the policy document, whether received electronically or
otherwise,  You  have  an  option  to  review  the  policy  terms  and
conditions. If You are not satisfied or have any disagreement with the
terms and conditions of the Policy or otherwise and have not made
any claim, the Policy Document needs to be returned to the Company
with reasons for cancellation within 30 days from the date of receipt
of  the  Policy  Document.  We  will  refund  the  premium  paid  after
deduction of Stamp duty, proportionate risk premium for the period of

question: To which parties can benefits be legally payable according to Part-D Section 9 of the document?
ground_truth: Benefits are payable to the Policyholder, to the Assignee(s), or to the nominee where an endorsement has been recorded.
vectorRAG_answer:
According to the provided document sections, benefits are payable to the following parties: 
1. The Policyholder 
2. The Assignee(s)
3. The nominee, where an endorsement has been recorded in accordance with Section 38 and Section 39 of the Insurance Act, 1938 [Page 3]. 

In the event of the death of the Policyholder, benefits may also be payable to the guardian of the minor Life Assured until the minor attains the age of majority, after which the policy ownership is changed [Page 3]. 

This information is found in Passage 2 and Passage 3 of the retrieved context.
graphRAG_answer:
According to the knowledge graph, benefits can be payable to the following parties:

* Policyholder or Assignee(s) [ENTITY] (as stated in triplet [2]: (Benefits [INSURANCE BENEFITS]) —[Are Payable To] {source_page=3, source_chunk=26, confidence=0.95}→ (Policyholder or Assignee(s) [ENTITY]))
* Life Assured [PERSON] (as stated in triplet [11]: (benefits [BENEFIT]) —[Is Entitled To] {source_page=3, source_chunk=27, confidence=0.95}→ (Life Assured [PERSON]))
* Policyholder [POLICY ROLE] (as stated in triplet [9]: (benefits [BENEFIT]) —[Is Entitled To] {source_page=3, source_chunk=27, confidence=0.95}→ (Policyholder [POLICY ROLE]))

These relationships indicate that benefits are payable to the policyholder, assignees, life assured, and policyholder, as per the given triplets.
hybridRAG_answer: Benefits can be payable to the policyholder, assignee(s), and the life assured. In the event of the policyholder's death, benefits may also be payable to the guardian of a minor life assured until they reach the age of majority.

only the Paid -up Maturity Benefit (if applicable) is payable and the
Paid-up Death Benefit shall not be payable.

shall  become  payable.  Reversionary  bonuses  and  contingent
reversionary bonus is described in Part D, section 3. ix.On payment of
this paid-up maturity benefit to You, the policy will terminate and all
rights,  benefits  and  interests  under  the  policy  will  stand
extinguished.

9.Policy on the life of a Minor
If the policy has been taken on the life of a minor, on attaining the age
of majority i.e. 18 years, the policy will vest on him/her. Thereafter, the
Life Assured shall become the policyholder who will then be entitled
to all the benefits and subject to all liabilities as per the terms and
conditions  of  the  policy.  The  Life  Assured  cum  Policyholder  can", "metadata": {"source_file": "4ec93fc12bff.pdf", "page_number": 3, "chunk_index": 28, "page_chunk_index": 6}, "score": 0.1101}, {"text": "disclosed to policyholders.
9.To whom benefits are payable
Benefits  are  payable  to  the  Policyholder  or  to  the  Assignee(s),
nominee where an endorsement has been recorded in accordance
with Section 38 and Section 39 of the Insurance Act, 1938 as maybe
applicable. If the Policyholder and the Life Assured are different, then
in  the  event  of  death  of  the  Policyholder  and  upon  subsequent
intimation of the death with the Company: • If the Life Assured is a
minor: the policy shall vest on the guardian of the minor life assured
till he/she attains the age of majority. Upon attaining the age of
majority the ownership of the policy shall be changed according to
Clause 9 below; • If the Life Assured is major: the policy shall vest on
the  Life  Assured.  Thereafter,  the  Life  Assured  shall  become  the

conditions  of  the  policy.  The  Life  Assured  cum  Policyholder  can

as amended from time to time. However, if the policy is assigned
during the minority of the Life Assured, then the vesting of the policy
shall be kept in abeyance till the assignment is valid and effective.

This section is not applicable to Your policy

General Conditions

correct. However, if at any point of time it is found that the age of the

actual Age of the Life Assured, then the Company reserves the right
to cancel the Policy.

question: For a policy with a Premium Payment Term of 10 years, what is the Guaranteed Additions (GAs) rate for policy years 11 to 15?
ground_truth: The GA rate is 15% of the annualized premium for policy years 11 to 15 when the PPT is 10 years.
vectorRAG_answer:

i. On survival of the Life Assured till the Date of Maturity, for a fully
paid policy, the following will be payable: Maturity Benefit = Higher of
(D,  E) Where,  D  =  Guaranteed  Maturity  Benefit  (GMB)  +  accrued

declared  + terminal bonus, if declared E = 100.1% X (annualised
premium) ii. The Guaranteed Maturity Benefit (GMB) will be set at
policy  inception  and  is  as  mentioned  in  the  policy  schedule. iii.
Maturity benefit may be taxable as per the prevailing tax laws. iv. On
payment of Maturity Benefit to You, the policy will terminate and all
rights, benefits and interests under the policy will stand extinguished.

question: If the 10-year Government Securities yield is sourced at 6.50%, what will be the applicable policy loan interest rate?
ground_truth: The interest rate will be 8.00% (since the policy specifies it is equal to 150 basis points, which is 1.50%, in addition to the prevailing 10-year G-Sec yield).
vectorRAG_answer:
The applicable policy loan interest rate can be calculated based on the information provided in the context. According to [Passage 1], the applicable interest rate will be equal to 150 basis points in addition to the prevailing yield on 10-year Government Securities. 

Given the 10-year Government Securities yield is 6.50%, we can calculate the interest rate as follows: 
6.50% + 150 basis points (which is 1.50%) = 8.00%. 

This calculation is consistent with the information provided in [Passage 1], where it is stated that "The loan interest rate in September 2025 is 8.00% p.a. compounded half-yearly."

Therefore, if the 10-year Government Securities yield is sourced at 6.50%, the applicable policy loan interest rate would be 8.00% p.a. compounded half-yearly, as per [Passage 1].

6.Renewal Premium in Advance
Collection of renewal premium in advance shall be allowed within the
same  financial  year  for  the  premium  due  in  that  financial  year.
However, where the renewal premium due in one financial year is
being collected in advance in earlier financial year, we may collect the
same for a maximum period of three months in advance from the due
date of the premium. The renewal premium so collected in advance
shall only be adjusted on the due date of the premium.

question: Write out the full mathematical formula for calculating the Guaranteed Surrender Value (GSV).
ground_truth: GSV = (GSV Factor X Total premiums paid) + 30% x (Vested reversionary bonuses + Accrued Guaranteed Additions).
vectorRAG_answer:
The full mathematical formula for calculating the Guaranteed Surrender Value (GSV) is: 
GSV = GSV Factor X Total premiums paid plus 30% x (Vested reversionary bonuses + Accrued Guaranteed Additions) [Page 2]. 

The GSV factors are as follows:
- Policy Year of Surrender 1: 15%
- Policy Year of Surrender 2: 30%
- Policy Year of Surrender 3: 35%
- Policy Year of Surrender 4 to 7: 50%
- Policy Year of Surrender 8 to (Policy Term less 2): 50% + 40% × (Policy Year – 7) ÷ (Policy Term – 8)
- Policy Year of Surrender Policy Term less 1 to Policy Term: 90% [Page 2]. 

Note that the exact policy term and other specific details may affect the calculation, but the basic formula and factors are provided above.

question: Using the formula for policy years 8 to (Policy Term less 2), calculate the GSV factor for policy year 10 when the total policy term is 15 years.
ground_truth: Using the formula: 50% + 40% x (Policy Year - 7) / (Policy Term - 8). Substituting Year = 10 and Term = 15: 50% + 40% x (10 - 7) / (15 - 8) = 50% + 40% x 3 / 7 = 50% + 17.14% = 67.14%.

According to the table, for policy years 8 to (Policy Term less 2), the GSV factor is 50%. Since the total policy term is 15 years, "Policy Term less 2" would be 15 - 2 = 13. Therefore, policy year 10 falls within the range of 8 to 13, and the applicable GSV factor is 50%.

question: How does converting a policy to 'paid-up' status affect future reversionary bonuses, and what happens to missed bonuses if the policy is subsequently revived?
ground_truth: Once a policy becomes paid-up, no future reversionary or terminal bonuses accrue. However, upon revival, all applicable reversionary bonuses and GAs declared since premium discontinuance up to the date of revival will accrue to the policy, and any contingent reversionary bonus attached will be reversed.
vectorRAG_answer:
Converting a policy to 'paid-up' status affects future reversionary bonuses in that no future bonuses shall accrue [Page 3]. However, if the policy is subsequently revived, all applicable Guaranteed Additions (GAs) and reversionary bonuses declared since premium discontinuance up to the date of revival shall accrue to the policy [Page 2]. Additionally, the contingent reversionary bonus attached to the policy will be reversed upon revival [Page 2]. 

It is also stated that a paid-up policy will not be entitled to future reversionary bonuses or terminal bonus, if declared [Page 2]. 

Sources: [Page 2], [Page 3]
graphRAG_answer:
Converting a policy to 'paid-up' status affects future reversionary bonuses as a paid-up policy is not entitled to future reversionary bonuses, as stated in triplet [1] (Paid-up Policy [POLICY TYPE]) —[Is Not Entitled To]→ (Future Reversionary Bonuses [INSURANCE BONUS TYPE]). Additionally, triplet [3] (Paid-up policy [POLICY STATUS]) —[Has No Future Bonuses]→ (Future bonuses [POLICY BENEFIT]) indicates that a paid-up policy has no future bonuses.

If the policy is subsequently revived, the knowledge graph does not provide information on what happens to missed bonuses. However, triplet [29] (Active Policy [INSURANCE POLICY STATUS]) —[Revives To]→ (Paid-up Policy [POLICY TYPE]) suggests that an active policy can revive to a paid-up policy, but it does not mention the treatment of missed bonuses.

⚠️ The requested information on missed bonuses during the revival of a paid-up policy was not found in the knowledge graph. The graph may not contain this specific relationship.
hybridRAG_answer: Converting a policy to 'paid-up' status means it will not be entitled to future reversionary bonuses. If the policy is later revived, all applicable bonuses that were missed while it was paid-up will be added to the policy.

question: Compare the foreclosure rules for an active/fully paid policy with those for a policy that is not active or fully paid when a loan is outstanding.
ground_truth: For policies other than in-force and fully paid-up, if the outstanding loan amount including interest exceeds the Surrender Value, the policy will be foreclosed (after due notice). For in-force and/or fully paid-up policies, the policy cannot be foreclosed on the ground of the outstanding loan amount exceeding the Surrender Value.
vectorRAG_answer:
For an active/fully paid policy, if a loan is outstanding, the policy cannot be foreclosed on the ground of outstanding loan amount, including interest, exceeding the Surrender Value [Page 3]. 

In contrast, for a policy that is not active or fully paid, if the outstanding loan amount, including interest, exceeds the Surrender Value, the policy will be foreclosed [Page 3]. The policyholder will be given due intimation/notice prior to the policy foreclosure as a reasonable opportunity for continuing the policy.

It is also stated that on foreclosure, the policy will terminate, and all rights, benefits, and interests under the policy will stand extinguished [Page 3]. 

These rules are outlined in the sections discussing loan provisions and policy foreclosure [Page 3].
graphRAG_answer:

On the other hand, triplet [5] states that "Policy [INSURANCE POLICY]" —[Will Be Foreclosed If]→ "Outstanding loan amount [FINANCIAL OBLIGATION]". This suggests that a policy can be foreclosed if there is an outstanding loan amount, but it does not specify the policy type.

Triplet [20] shows that "Policy [INSURANCE POLICY]" —[Requires Assignment Of]→ "Loan [FINANCIAL PRODUCT]", implying that a policy can be assigned as a loan. However, it does not differentiate between active/fully paid policies and those that are not.

From triplet [4] and [7], we understand that an "Active Policy [INSURANCE POLICY STATUS]" can revive to a "Paid-up Policy [POLICY TYPE]" and vice versa. This relationship highlights the connection between active and paid-up policies but does not directly address foreclosure rules.

Given the information in the knowledge graph, we can conclude that fully paid-up policies have specific protection against foreclosure when a loan is outstanding, as stated in triplet [1]. However, the foreclosure rules for policies that are not active or fully paid are less clear, with triplet [5] suggesting that any policy can be foreclosed if there is an outstanding loan amount, without specifying the policy type.

question: What additional documents are required to process a death claim for an unnatural death compared to a natural death?
ground_truth: An unnatural death claim requires all standard natural death documents plus: Post Mortem report & viscera/chemical analysis report, FIR report, final police investigation report, police panchnama/Inquest report, and a driving license.
vectorRAG_answer:
To process a death claim for an unnatural death, the following additional documents are required compared to a natural death: 

- Post Mortem report & viscera/ chemical analysis report 
- FIR report, final police investigation report, police panchnama/ Inquest report, driving license 

These requirements are stated in [Passage 1, Page 4] for unnatural deaths, which are not mentioned in the list for natural deaths in [Passage 4, Page 3] and [Passage 1, Page 4]. 

Note that while [Passage 2, Page 3] and [Passage 5, Page 4] provide some information on death claims, the specific comparison between natural and unnatural death claims is primarily found in [Passage 1, Page 4].

question: Explain the vesting rules when a policy is taken on a minor's life, including what happens when they turn 18 and how a policy assignment affects this process.
ground_truth: On reaching 18, the policy vests in the minor who becomes the policyholder, gaining all benefits, liabilities, and the right to nominate. However, if the policy was assigned during their minority, the vesting of the policy is kept in abeyance till the assignment is valid and effective.
vectorRAG_answer:
When a policy is taken on a minor's life, the policy will vest on them when they attain the age of majority, i.e., 18 years [Page 3]. Thereafter, the Life Assured shall become the policyholder who will then be entitled to all the benefits and subject to all liabilities as per the terms and conditions of the policy [Page 3]. 

However, if the policy is assigned during the minority of the Life Assured, then the vesting of the policy shall be kept in abeyance till the assignment is valid and effective [Page 3]. 

In the event of the policyholder's death, if the Life Assured is a minor, the policy shall vest on the guardian of the minor life assured till they attain the age of majority [Page 3]. Upon attaining the age of majority, the ownership of the policy shall be changed according to the specified clause [Page 3]. 

It is also mentioned that the Life Assured cum Policyholder can register due nomination as per Section 39 of the Insurance Act, 1938, as amended from time to time [Page 3]. 

The assignment of a policy should be in accordance with Section 38 of the Insurance Act, 1938, as amended from time to time [Page 6]. The assignment must be signed by the transferor or assignor or duly authorized agent and attested by at least one witness [Page 6]. The transfer of assignment shall not be operative as against an insurer until a notice in writing of the transfer is delivered to the insurer [Page 6].

question: What is the general rule regarding a registered nomination when a policy is assigned under Section 38, and what are the specific loan-related exceptions to this rule?
ground_truth: Generally, a transfer or assignment under Section 38 automatically cancels a nomination. The exception is an assignment to the insurer or other transferee/assignee for a loan or against security; in this case, the nomination is not cancelled to the extent of the insurer's/assignee's interest and will fully revive once the loan is repaid.
vectorRAG_answer: According to the provided context, the general rule regarding a registered nomination when a policy is assigned under Section 38 is that the nomination will automatically cancel, except in specific cases [Page 6]. The exceptions to this rule are when the assignment is made to the insurer or other transferee or assignee for the purpose of a loan or against security, or its reassignment after repayment [Page 6]. In such cases, the nomination will not get cancelled to the extent of the insurer's or transferee's or assignee's interest in the policy, and the nomination will revive on repayment of the loan [Page 6].
graphRAG_answer:
According to the knowledge graph, when a policy is assigned under Section 38, the general rule regarding a registered nomination is that it can be cancelled or changed by the policyholder (Triplets [12] and [18]). 

The specific loan-related exceptions to this rule are:
- A loan on a policy requires assignment of the policy (Triplet [7] and [13]), 
- A loan is not entitled to a conditional assignee (Triplet [16]), 
- Repayment of a loan can revive a nomination (Triplet [26] and [29]).

These relationships indicate that while a nomination can generally be cancelled or changed, there are specific considerations and potential revivals related to loan transactions on a policy. 

The relevant entities and relationships used to form this answer include:
- Policy [INSURANCE POLICY]
- Nomination [ACTION]
- Policyholder [POLICY ROLE]
- Loan [FINANCIAL PRODUCT]
- Section 38 of the Insurance Act, 1938 [LEGISLATION SECTION]
- Repayment of loan [FINANCIAL TRANSACTION]
- Conditional Assignee [ENTITY]
hybridRAG_answer: When a policy is assigned under Section 38, the general rule is that a registered nomination will automatically cancel. However, there are exceptions related to loans, where the nomination will not be cancelled if the assignment is for a loan or security purpose. In such cases, the nomination will remain in effect to the extent of the lender's interest and will revive once the loan is repaid.

question: If a policyholder commits suicide, how does the payout structure change if it happens within 12 months of risk commencement versus within 12 months of a policy revival?
ground_truth: The payout structure is the same in both scenarios: the company pays the higher of 80% of the Total Premiums Paid till the date of death or the surrender value available till the date of death, and the policy is subsequently terminated.
vectorRAG_answer:
According to the provided context, if a policyholder commits suicide within 12 months of risk commencement, the payout will be the "higher of 80% of Total Premiums Paid, if any till the date of death or the surrender value as available on till the date of death" [Page 4]. 

In the case of a policy revival, if the policyholder commits suicide within 12 months of the revival, the payout structure remains the same, which is the "higher of 80% of the Total Premiums Paid, if any till the date of death or surrender value as available on death" [Page 4].

The payout structure does not change based on whether the suicide occurs within 12 months of risk commencement or within 12 months of a policy revival, as both scenarios result in the same payout calculation [Page 4].

question: How is the policy loan interest rate calculated, how frequently is it reviewed, and on what calendar day does any interest rate change take effect?
ground_truth: The loan interest rate is calculated as 150 basis points plus the prevailing yield on 10-year Government Securities (sourced from Bloomberg). It is reviewed monthly by the company, and any rate change becomes effective on the 15th of the month.
vectorRAG_answer:
The policy loan interest rate is calculated as 150 basis points in addition to the prevailing yield on 10-year Government Securities, which is sourced from www.bloomberg.com. As of September 2025, the interest rate is 8.00% p.a. compounded half-yearly [Page 3]. 

The loan interest rate is reviewed monthly by the company [Page 3]. Any change in the interest rate shall be effective from the 15th of the month [Page 3]. 

Sources: [Passage 1, Page 3], [Passage 2, Page 3], [Passage 3, Page 3], [Passage 4, Page 3].

question: Under Section 38, what are the four grounds on which an insurer can decline to act on a policy transfer, and what is the timeline for communicating this refusal?
ground_truth: An insurer can decline an assignment if they have reasons to believe it is: (a) not bona fide, (b) not in the interest of the policyholder, (c) not in public interest, or (d) is for trading. The reasons must be recorded and communicated in writing to the policyholder within 30 days of receiving the assignment notice.
vectorRAG_answer:
Under Section 38, the four grounds on which an insurer can decline to act on a policy transfer are:
a. not bonafide 
b. not in the interest of the policyholder 
c. not in public interest 
d. is for the purpose of trading of the insurance policy.

The insurer should record the reasons in writing and communicate the same in writing to the Policyholder within 30 days from the date of policyholder giving a notice of transfer or assignment [Page 6]. 

In case of refusal to act upon the endorsement by the Insurer, any person aggrieved by the refusal may prefer a claim to IRDAI within 30 days of receipt of the refusal letter from the Insurer [Page 6].

question: How does the grace period differ across premium frequencies, and what happens to the death benefit if the life assured dies during this window?
ground_truth: The grace period is 15 days for monthly frequency and 30 days for all other frequencies. If the Life Assured dies during the grace period, the life cover continues, and the company will pay the full Death Benefit as per the policy terms and conditions.
vectorRAG_answer:
The grace period differs across premium frequencies in the following manner: if the policyholder has chosen a monthly frequency, they will be given a grace period of 15 days for payment of due instalment premium, whereas if they have chosen any other frequency, they will be given a grace period of 30 days for payment of due instalment premium [Page 2].

If the life assured dies during the grace period, the death benefit will be paid as per the terms and conditions of the policy [Page 2]. This means that the life cover continues during the grace period, and the policy will pay the death benefit if the life assured dies during this time.
graphRAG_answer:
The grace period is given to the policyholder (triplet [11]) and continues during the life cover (triplet [10]). However, the graph does not explicitly state how the grace period differs across premium frequencies. 

Regarding the death benefit, if the life assured dies during the grace period, the death benefit may be triggered (triplet [1] and triplet [3]). The death benefit equals the higher of (A,B) (triplet [20]).

If you pay monthly, you have 15 days to make a payment after it's due, but for other payment schedules, you have 30 days. If the person insured dies during this time, the death benefit will still be paid according to the policy's terms, meaning the life cover remains in effect during the grace period.

question: What are the specific conditions, time limits, and late payment interest rate calculations required for a policyholder to revive a discontinued policy?
ground_truth: The revival application must be made within 5 consecutive years from the first unpaid premium and before the policy's maturity. The policyholder must supply satisfactory proof of health (at their own expense) and pay premium arrears with interest. The interest rate is reviewed monthly, set on the 15th, and is equal to 150 basis points plus the 10-year G-Sec yield.
vectorRAG_answer:
To revive a discontinued policy, the policyholder must meet the following conditions and time limits:

1. **Time Limit**: The application for revival must be made within 5 consecutive years from the due date of the first unpaid premium and before the termination date of the Policy [Page 3].
2. **Health Evidence**: The policyholder must furnish, at their own expense, satisfactory evidence of health as required by the company [Page 3].
3. **Arrears of Premiums**: The arrears of premiums together with interest must be paid [Page 3].
4. **Interest Rate**: The interest rate applicable for late payment of premiums is 8.00% p.a. compounded half yearly, as of September 2025 [Page 3]. The revival interest rate is calculated as 150 basis points in addition to the prevailing yield on 10-year Government Securities, which is sourced from www.bloomberg.com [Page 3].

The revival of the policy may be on terms different from those applicable to the policy before premiums were discontinued, and the company reserves the right to refuse to revive the policy [Page 3]. The revival will take effect only if it is specifically communicated by the company to the policyholder [Page 3]. 

The revival interest rate will be reviewed on the 15th day of every month by the company based on the 10-year G-Sec yield of one day prior to such review [Page 3]. 

Note that additional conditions, such as extra mortality premiums or charges, may be applicable for policy revival [Page 3].

2.Surrender Value

full year’s Premiums. Prior to receipt of one full year’s premium, no
surrender value is payable. On surrender of the policy, we will pay the
surrender value equal to the higher of: • Guaranteed Surrender Value
(GSV) • Special Surrender Value (SSV) The policy will terminate on
payment of the surrender value. Surrender will extinguish all rights,
benefits  and  interests  under  the  policy.  Surrender  value  may  be
taxable as per prevailing tax laws. a. Guaranteed Surrender Value
(GSV)  will  be  calculated  as  follows: GSV  =  GSV  Factor  X  Total
premiums paid plus 30% x (Vested reversionary bonuses + Accrued

2.Surrender Value
You can Surrender the policy any time after payment of at least one
full year’s Premiums. Prior to receipt of one full year’s premium, no
surrender value is payable. On surrender of the policy, we will pay the
surrender value equal to the higher of: • Guaranteed Surrender Value

same  financial  year  for  the  premium  due  in  that  financial  year.

being collected in advance in earlier financial year, we may collect the
same for a maximum period of three months in advance from the due
date of the premium. The renewal premium so collected in advance
shall only be adjusted on the due date of the premium.

question: What is the administrative fee and refund policy if a customer decides to cancel their policy after the 30-day Free Look period has passed?
ground_truth: The document does not provide a refund option or specify any administrative fees for cancellation after the 30-day Free Look period; it only mentions standard surrender values if at least one full year's premiums are paid.
vectorRAG_answer:
The administrative fee and refund policy after the 30-day Free Look period has passed is related to the surrender value of the policy. According to [Passage 2], if the customer surrenders the policy after paying at least one full year's premiums, they will receive a surrender value equal to the higher of the Guaranteed Surrender Value (GSV) or Special Surrender Value (SSV). The GSV is calculated based on the GSV factor, which varies depending on the policy year of surrender. 

However, the specific administrative fee for surrendering the policy is not mentioned in the provided context. The only fee mentioned is the charge for issuing a duplicate policy document, which is ₹ 200, as stated in [Passage 3] and [Passage 5].

Regarding the refund policy, [Passage 1] states that if the customer cancels the policy within the 30-day Free Look period, they will receive a refund of the premium paid after deduction of Stamp duty, proportionate risk premium for the period of cover, and the expenses borne by the company on medical tests, if any. But for cancellations after the Free Look period, the refund policy is tied to the surrender value, as mentioned in [Passage 2].

PART-D
1.Free look Period (30 days refund policy)
On receipt of the policy document, whether received electronically or
otherwise,  You  have  an  option  to  review  the  policy  terms  and
conditions. If You are not satisfied or have any disagreement with the
terms and conditions of the Policy or otherwise and have not made
any claim, the Policy Document needs to be returned to the Company
with reasons for cancellation within 30 days from the date of receipt
of  the  Policy  Document.  We  will  refund  the  premium  paid  after
deduction of Stamp duty, proportionate risk premium for the period of
cover and the expenses borne by Us on medical tests, if any. The
Policy  shall  terminate  on  payment  of  this  amount  and  all  rights,
benefits and interests under this Policy will stand extinguished.", "metadata": {"source_file": "4ec93fc12bff.pdf", "page_number": 2, "chunk_index": 18, "page_chunk_index": 7}, "score": 0.1148}, {"text": "information/document(s) provided to Us in respect of the Proposal
Form,  and  any  endorsement  issued  by  Us. 18.Policy  Issue  Date
means the date as specified in the policy schedule. 19.Policy Month
means a period of one month from the Policy Acceptance Date and
every subsequent month thereafter. 20.Policy Term means the period
between  the  Policy  Acceptance  Date  and  the  Date  of  Maturity
specified in the Policy Schedule. 21.Policy Schedule means the policy
schedule and any endorsements attached to and forming part of this
Policy. 22.Policy Year means a period of 12 months commencing from
the Policy Acceptance Date and every policy anniversary thereafter.
23.Premium  means the instalment premium specified in the Policy
Schedule (exclusive of taxes) which is payable/has been received
under  the  Policy. 24.Premium  Payment  Term means  the  period
specified in the Policy Schedule during which Premium is payable.", "metadata": {"source_file": "4ec93fc12bff.pdf", "page_number": 1, "chunk_index": 3, "page_chunk_index": 3}, "score": 0.1103}, {"text": "Schedule (exclusive of taxes) which is payable/has been received
under  the  Policy. 24.Premium  Payment  Term means  the  period
specified in the Policy Schedule during which Premium is payable.
25.Premium Paying policy means a policy which is neither a paid-up
nor fully paid policy. 26.Proposal Form a form to be filled in by You in
physical or electronic form, for furnishing the information including
material information, if any, as required by Us in respect of a risk, in
order  to  enable  Us  to  take  informed  decision  in  the  context  of
underwriting the risk, and in the event of acceptance of the risk, to
determine the rates, advantages, terms and conditions of the cover to

authority  that  has  regulatory  jurisdiction  and  powers  over  Us.
Currently the Regulator is the Insurance Regulatory and Development
Authority of India (IRDAI). 28.Revival of the Policy means restoration
of the policy, which was discontinued due to the non-payment of
premium, by the insurer with all the benefits mentioned in the policy
document, with or without rider benefits if any, upon the receipt of all
the premiums due and other charges or late fee if any, during the
Revival Period, as per the terms and conditions of the Policy, upon
being satisfied as to the continued insurability of Life Assured on the
basis of the information, documents and reports furnished by the
policyholder, in accordance with Board approved underwriting policy..
29.Revival  Period  means  the  period  of  five  consecutive  complete
years from the date of discontinuance of the Policy, during which", "metadata": {"source_file": "4ec93fc12bff.pdf", "page_number": 1, "chunk_index": 5, "page_chunk_index": 5}, "score": 0.1067}, {"text": "Regulator.
8.Revival
A Policy which has discontinued payment of Premium may be revived
subject  to  underwriting  and  the  following  conditions: a)  The
application for revival is made within 5 consecutive years from the
due date of the first unpaid premium and before the termination date
of  the  Policy.  Revival  will  be  based  on  the  prevailing  Company
policy. b) You furnish, at your own expense, satisfactory evidence of
health as required by Us. c) The arrears of Premiums together with
interest at such rate as We may charge for late payment of premiums
are paid, based on the prevailing Company policy. Revival interest
rates will be set monthly and is equal to 150 basis points in addition
to the prevailing yield on 10-year Government Securities. The yield on
10-year  Government  Securities  will  be  sourced  from
www.bloomberg.com.The interest rate applicable in September 2025

question: I just got my policy document yesterday but I changed my mind and want to cancel it. Can I get all my money back, and how long do I have to submit this request?
ground_truth: Yes, you can request a cancellation within 30 days of receiving the document (the Free Look period). You will get a refund of the premium paid after deductions for stamp duty, proportionate risk premium for the coverage period, and any medical test expenses incurred by the company.
vectorRAG_answer:
You can cancel your policy within 30 days from the date of receipt of the Policy Document, as stated in the "Free look Period (30 days refund policy)" section [Page 2]. However, you will not get all your money back. The refund will be made after deduction of "Stamp duty, proportionate risk premium for the period of cover and the expenses borne by Us on medical tests, if any" [Page 2]. 

To cancel, you need to return the Policy Document to the Company with reasons for cancellation within the specified 30-day period. The Policy shall terminate on payment of this amount, and all rights, benefits, and interests under the Policy will stand extinguished [Page 2].
graphRAG_answer:
To address your question, we need to examine the relationships related to policy cancellation and refund. 

From triplet [8], we see that the Policy [INSURANCE POLICY] —[Has Free Look Period]→ (30 days [TIME PERIOD]), indicating you have 30 days to review and potentially cancel your policy.

Regarding the refund, triplet [25] states that the Policy [INSURANCE POLICY] —[Terminates On]→ (payment of refund [EVENT]), suggesting that upon termination (cancellation in this context), there is a provision for a refund. However, the exact amount of the refund is not specified in the provided graph context.

Therefore, you can cancel your policy within the 30-day free look period, and there is a provision for a refund upon cancellation. However, the specifics of the refund amount are not detailed in the graph.

⚠️ The requested information on the exact refund amount was not found in the knowledge graph. The graph may not contain this specific relationship.

- Policy [INSURANCE POLICY]
- Free Look Period
- 30 days [TIME PERIOD]
- payment of refund [EVENT]
hybridRAG_answer: You can cancel your policy within 30 days from the date you received it. To do this, you need to return the policy document with reasons for cancellation. You will get a refund, but not all your money back, as some costs like stamp duty and medical test expenses will be deducted.

question: I missed my monthly premium payment date because I was traveling. Will my life cover stop immediately, or do I have some extra time to pay?
ground_truth: Your life cover will not stop immediately. Since you are on a monthly payment schedule, you have a 15-day grace period from the premium due date. The life cover remains active, and if any unfortunate event occurs during this window, the death benefit is still payable.
vectorRAG_answer:
According to the provided context, if you miss your monthly premium payment date, you will be given a grace period to pay the due instalment premium. For monthly frequency, the grace period is 15 days [Page 2]. This means your life cover will continue during the grace period. In case of death during the grace period, the death benefit will be paid as per the policy terms and conditions [Page 2]. 

Therefore, your life cover will not stop immediately, and you have 15 days to pay the missed monthly premium.

question: My policy has been in a reduced 'paid-up' state for the last two years. Will I still receive the annual reversionary bonuses declared by the company?
ground_truth: No, once a policy is converted into a paid-up policy, it is no longer entitled to any future reversionary or terminal bonuses.
vectorRAG_answer:
According to the provided context, a paid-up policy will not be entitled to future reversionary bonuses or terminal bonus, if declared [Page 2]. This information is found in Passage 2, which states: "iv.A paid-up policy will not be entitled to future reversionary bonuses or terminal bonus ,if declared."

Therefore, since your policy has been in a reduced 'paid-up' state for the last two years, you will not receive the annual reversionary bonuses declared by the company.

Source: [Passage 2] — Source: 4ec93fc12bff.pdf, Page 2
graphRAG_answer:
Based on the knowledge graph triplets, a paid-up policy is not entitled to future reversionary bonuses. This is stated in triplet [2] (Paid-up Policy [POLICY TYPE]) —[Is Not Entitled To]→ (Future Reversionary Bonuses [INSURANCE BONUS TYPE]). 

However, triplet [4] (paid-up policies [POLICY TYPE]) —[Accrues To]→ (Contingent Reversionary Bonus [INSURANCE BONUS TYPE]) indicates that paid-up policies do accrue contingent reversionary bonuses. 

Therefore, as a policyholder with a paid-up policy, you will not receive future reversionary bonuses, but you may be eligible for contingent reversionary bonuses, as per triplets [2] and [4]. 

Note: The graph does not provide information on the annual frequency of reversionary bonuses, but rather the eligibility of paid-up policies to receive them. 

Referenced entities and relationships: 
- Paid-up Policy [POLICY TYPE]
- Future Reversionary Bonuses [INSURANCE BONUS TYPE]
- Contingent Reversionary Bonus [INSURANCE BONUS TYPE]
- Triplets [2] and [4]
hybridRAG_answer: You will not receive the annual reversionary bonuses declared by the company because a paid-up policy is not entitled to future reversionary bonuses. However, you may be eligible for contingent reversionary bonuses.

question: I am facing a financial emergency and need some quick cash. Can I get a loan from my policy, and how much can I borrow?
ground_truth: Yes, you can borrow against your policy as long as it has acquired a positive Surrender Value. The maximum loan amount you can get is up to 80% of the policy's current Surrender Value.
vectorRAG_answer:
You can get a loan from your policy, provided a positive Surrender Value is payable under the Policy at the time of disbursement of the same [Page 3]. The loan amount that can be availed is up to 80% of the Surrender value [Page 3]. 

To be eligible for a loan, your policy must have a Surrender Value, which can be determined after payment of at least one full year’s Premiums [Page 2]. The Surrender Value is equal to the higher of the Guaranteed Surrender Value (GSV) or Special Surrender Value (SSV) [Page 2]. 

The applicable interest rate for the loan will be equal to 150 basis points in addition to the prevailing yield on 10-year Government Securities [Page 3]. As of September 2025, the loan interest rate is 8.00% p.a. compounded half-yearly [Page 3].
graphRAG_answer:
You can get a loan from your policy. The amount you can borrow is limited to the surrender value of your policy, as stated in triplet [16]: (Loan [FINANCIAL PRODUCT]) —[Is Limited To] {source_page=3, source_chunk=22, confidence=0.95}→ (Surrender value [POLICY VALUE])  [Page 3]. 

Additionally, triplet [1] confirms that your policy has loan availability: (Policy [INSURANCE POLICY]) —[Has Loan Availability] {source_page=3, source_chunk=22, confidence=0.95}→ (Loan [FINANCIAL PRODUCT])  [Page 3]. 

However, the exact surrender value is not specified in the provided context. It is mentioned in triplet [26] as (Surrender Value [MONETARY VALUE]), but the value is not given.
hybridRAG_answer: You can get a loan from your policy if it has a surrender value. The loan amount is limited to up to 80% of the surrender value. To be eligible, your policy must have a surrender value, which is usually available after paying at least one year's premiums. The surrender value is the higher of the Guaranteed Surrender Value or Special Surrender Value.

and the balance amount will be payable. f.For availing this feature of

rate will be equal to 150 basis points in addition to the prevailing
yield  on  10  year  Government  Securities.  The  yield  on  10-year
Government Securities will be sourced from  www.bloomberg.com.

(excluding any extra mortality premium, service tax and cesses).
4.Loan
a.Loans are available provided a positive Surrender Value is payable
under the Policy at the time of disbursement of the same. .  b.Loan
amount of up to 80% of the Surrender value can be availed.  c.For
other than in-force and fully paid-up policies, if the outstanding loan
amount including interest exceeds the Surrender Value, the policy will
be Foreclosed. You shall be given due intimation/ notice prior to the

2.Surrender Value
You can Surrender the policy any time after payment of at least one
full year’s Premiums. Prior to receipt of one full year’s premium, no
surrender value is payable. On surrender of the policy, we will pay the
surrender value equal to the higher of: • Guaranteed Surrender Value
(GSV) • Special Surrender Value (SSV) The policy will terminate on
payment of the surrender value. Surrender will extinguish all rights,
benefits  and  interests  under  the  policy.  Surrender  value  may  be
taxable as per prevailing tax laws. a. Guaranteed Surrender Value
(GSV)  will  be  calculated  as  follows: GSV  =  GSV  Factor  X  Total
premiums paid plus 30% x (Vested reversionary bonuses + Accrued
Guaranteed Additions).  GSV factors shall be as follows:

payment and all rights, benefits and interests under the Policy will

12.Issue of duplicate policy  We shall issue a duplicate of Policy
document, on receipt of a written request for the same from You
along with the necessary documents as may be required by Us and
at such charges as may be applicable from time to time. The current

not available on issue of duplicate Policy document.

question: I paid my renewal premium by cheque, but it was returned due to insufficient funds. Am I safe as long as I have the payment slip?
ground_truth: No, you are not safe. The policy terms specify that you are solely responsible for verifying that your payment goes through. If a payment is not realized, the premium is not considered paid, and if it was the first premium, the policy stands automatically cancelled.
vectorRAG_answer:
According to the provided context, if your cheque is returned due to insufficient funds, you are solely responsible for the verification of such realization [Passage 1]. Additionally, if the payment made towards the renewal premium is not realized by ICICI Prudential Life Insurance Company Limited due to any reason whatsoever, you shall be solely responsible for the verification of such realization [Passage 2]. Having a payment slip may not be sufficient, as the company emphasizes the importance of the payment being realized.

It is also stated that if the payment made towards the first premium or renewal premium is not realized by the company due to any reason whatsoever, you shall be solely responsible for the verification of such realization [Passage 1]. Therefore, having a payment slip does not guarantee that you are safe, as the payment needs to be realized by the company.

Source: [Passage 1] and [Passage 2], which appear to be from Page 2 of the document.

question: My insurance agent offered to collect my renewal premium in cash. Is it okay to pay him directly?
ground_truth: No, you should not pay him in cash. The policy terms explicitly state that no person, individual, or entity is authorized to collect cash or self-cheques/bearer cheques on behalf of the company. Payments must be made through official channels.
vectorRAG_answer:
According to the provided document, specifically [Passage 1], it is stated that "viii. No person or individual or entity is authorized to collect cash or self-cheque or bearer cheque on Our behalf." This implies that it is not okay to pay your insurance agent directly in cash for your renewal premium.

Source: [Page 2]
graphRAG_answer:
According to the knowledge graph, there is no direct relationship that explicitly states it is okay to pay the renewal premium in cash directly to the insurance agent. 

However, triplet [4] (Premium [PAYMENT]) —[Can Be Paid Through] {source_page=2, source_chunk=14, confidence=0.9}→ (Direct Debit [PAYMENT METHOD]) and other similar triplets (5, 6, 11, 12, 13, 14, 20, 29) list various payment methods, but none of them mention paying in cash directly to the agent.

Additionally, triplet [1] (Renewal Premium [PAYMENT TYPE]) —[Can Be Collected] {source_page=2, source_chunk=17, confidence=0.95}→ (Financial Year [TIME PERIOD]) indicates that renewal premium can be collected, but it does not specify the payment method or the entity collecting it.

question: I have lost my original policy bond. Is there a way to get a new one, and will it cost me anything?
ground_truth: Yes, you can get a duplicate policy document by submitting a written request to the company along with the necessary documentation. There is an administrative charge of Rs. 200 for issuing a duplicate document.
vectorRAG_answer: You can obtain a duplicate policy document by submitting a written request along with the necessary documents. The current charge for issuance of a duplicate policy is ₹ 200. This information is found in [Passage 1, Page 4] and [Passage 2, Page 4]. Additionally, it is mentioned that the "Freelook option is not available on issue of duplicate Policy document."
graphRAG_answer:
To address your concern about losing the original policy bond, we can refer to triplet [2] which mentions a "Duplicate Policy" and its associated issuance charge. According to this triplet, a (Duplicate Policy [POLICY DOCUMENT]) —[Has Issuance Charge]→ (₹ 200 [MONETARY VALUE]). This implies that you can obtain a duplicate policy, and it will cost you ₹ 200.

Additionally, triplet [5] states that a (Duplicate Policy [POLICY DOCUMENT]) —[Does Not Have]→ (Freelook option [POLICY FEATURE]), but this does not directly pertain to the cost or the process of obtaining a duplicate policy.

Therefore, based on the provided knowledge graph, you can get a new policy bond as a duplicate, and it will cost you ₹ 200. 

Entities and relationships used: 
- Duplicate Policy [POLICY DOCUMENT]
- Has Issuance Charge
- ₹ 200 [MONETARY VALUE]
- Freelook option [POLICY FEATURE]
- Does Not Have 

Reference triplets: [2], [5]

question: I want to transfer my policy to my brother as a gift. What is the process for doing this, and how do I notify the insurance company?
ground_truth: You can transfer the policy through an assignment under Section 38. You must execute an endorsement on the policy itself or prepare a separate instrument indicating the reasons, your brother's details, and terms. It must be signed by you and attested by a witness. You must then deliver a written notice of assignment along with the certified instrument to the servicing branch.
vectorRAG_answer:
To transfer your policy to your brother as a gift, the process involves assigning or transferring the policy in accordance with Section 38 of the Insurance Act, 1938, as amended from time to time [Page 6]. The assignment may be effected by an endorsement upon the policy itself or by a separate instrument under notice to the Insurer [Page 6]. 

The instrument of assignment should indicate the fact of transfer or assignment, the reasons for the assignment or transfer, antecedents of the assignee, and terms on which assignment is made [Page 6]. The assignment must be signed by the transferor or assignor or duly authorized agent and attested by at least one witness [Page 6].

To notify the insurance company, a notice in writing of the transfer or assignment, along with the endorsement or instrument itself, or a copy thereof certified to be correct by both transferor and transferee, or their duly authorized agents, must be delivered to the insurer [Page 6]. The insurer should grant a written acknowledgement of receipt of notice, which shall be conclusive evidence against the insurer of duly receiving the notice [Page 6].

It is also mentioned that the transfer or assignment shall not be operative as against an insurer until a notice in writing of the transfer or assignment has been delivered to the insurer [Page 6]. 

Please note that the insurer may accept or decline to act upon any transfer or assignment or endorsement if it has sufficient reasons to believe that it is not bonafide or not in the interest of the policyholder or not in public interest or is for the purpose of trading of the insurance policy [Page 6].

question: I want to name my 12-year-old daughter as my policy beneficiary. What extra steps do I need to take since she is a minor?
ground_truth: Since your daughter is a minor, you must appoint an 'Appointee' (an adult) in the policy records. This appointee will be authorized to receive the policy benefits on her behalf in the event of your death during her minority.
vectorRAG_answer:
To name your 12-year-old daughter as your policy beneficiary, since she is a minor, you need to appoint a person to receive the money secured by the policy in the event of your death during her minority. This is stated in [Passage 2] —  Source: 4ec93fc12bff.pdf, Page 6. The manner of appointment is to be laid down by the insurer. 

Additionally, it is mentioned in [Passage 2] that nomination can be made at any time before the maturity of the policy and can be cancelled or changed at any time before the policy matures. 

It is also important to note that if the nominee (your daughter) survives you, the amount secured by the policy shall be paid to her, as stated in [Passage 5] —  Source: 4ec93fc12bff.pdf, Page 6.

To name your 12-year-old daughter as your policy beneficiary, you need to consider that she is a minor. According to the knowledge graph, a Policyholder [POLICY ROLE] —[Nominates] {source_page=6, source_chunk=58, confidence=0.9}→ (Child [RELATIVE])  [Page 6] as seen in triplet [2].

hybridRAG_answer: To name your 12-year-old daughter as your policy beneficiary, you need to appoint a person to receive the money if you die while she is still a minor. This person will manage the money until your daughter is an adult. You can make this appointment at any time before the policy matures, and you can also cancel or change it at any time before the policy matures.

question: If I am unhappy with how the insurance company's grievance officer resolved my complaint, where can I go next, and is there a deadline for doing so?
ground_truth: If you are unsatisfied, you can escalate the matter to the company's internal Grievance Redressal Committee or approach the IRDAI Grievance Cell (Bima Bharosa). You also have the right to file an official complaint with the Insurance Ombudsman in your area within one year of receiving the rejection or decision from the insurer.
vectorRAG_answer:
If you are unhappy with how the insurance company's grievance officer resolved your complaint, you can go to the Insurance Ombudsman next. According to [Passage 2, Page 4], the Insurance Ombudsman is established for redressal of grievances with respect to life insurance policies. 

The deadline for filing a complaint with the Insurance Ombudsman is within one year after the order of the insurer rejecting the representation is received, or after receipt of the decision of the insurer or insurance broker, or after expiry of a period of one month from the date of sending the written representation to the insurer or insurance broker if no reply is received [Passage 1, Page 5, and Passage 3, Page 5]. 

You can find the contact information and areas of jurisdiction for different Insurance Ombudsman offices in [Passage 5, Page 5].
graphRAG_answer:
If you are unhappy with how the insurance company's grievance officer resolved your complaint, you can go to the Insurance Ombudsman next. According to triplet [13], a complaint can be submitted to the Insurance Ombudsman. 

The deadline for doing so is within one year, as stated in triplet [30], which mentions that a complaint is made within one year.

{"vector_sources": [{"text": "b)The complaint is made within one year—
i.after the order of the insurer rejecting the representation is received;
or
ii.after receipt of decision of the insurer or insurance broker, as the
case may be which is not to the satisfaction of the complainant;
iii.after expiry of a period of one month from the date of sending the
written representation to the insurer or insurance broker, as the case
may be if the insurer or insurance broker, as the case may be named
fails to furnish reply to the complainant.
4.The Ombudsman shall be empowered to condone the delay in such
cases as he may consider necessary, after calling for objections of the
insurer or insurance broker, as the case may be against the proposed
condonation and after recording reasons for condoning the delay and
in case the delay is condoned, the date of condonation of delay shall
be  deemed  to  be  the  date  of  filing  of  the  complaint,  for  further
proceedings under these rules.", "metadata": {"source_file": "4ec93fc12bff.pdf", "page_number": 5, "chunk_index": 46, "page_chunk_index": 1}, "score": 0.1117}, {"text": "Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India
Survey No. 115/1, Financial District, Nanakramguda, Gachibowli,
Hyderabad, Telangana State – 500032
Insurance Ombudsman:
The Central Government has established an office of the Insurance
Ombudsman for redressal of grievances with respect to life insurance
policies. As per Insurance Ombudsman Rules, 2017 and Insurance
Ombudsman  (Amendment)  Rules,  2021,  the  Ombudsman  shall
receive and consider complaints or alleging deficiency in performance
required of an insurer (including its agents and intermediaries) or an
insurance broker, on any of the following grounds:
a.delay  in  settlement  of  claims,  beyond  the  time  specified  in  the
regulations,  framed  under  the  Insurance  Regulatory  and
Development Authority of India Act, 1999;
b.any partial or total repudiation of claims by the life insurer, General
insurer or the health insurer;
c.disputes over Premium paid or payable in terms of insurance policy;", "metadata": {"source_file": "4ec93fc12bff.pdf", "page_number": 4, "chunk_index": 41, "page_chunk_index": 7}, "score": 0.1117}, {"text": "complaint, supported by documents, the nature and extent of the loss
caused to the complainant and the relief sought from the Insurance
Ombudsman.
3.No complaint to the Insurance Ombudsman shall lie unless—
a)the complainant has made a representation in writing or through
electronic mail or online through website of the insurer or insurance
broker concerned or the insurer named in the complaint and—
i.either  the  insurer  or  insurance  broker,  as  the  case  may  be  had
rejected the complaint; or
ii.the complainant had not received any reply within a period of one
month after the insurer or insurance broker, as the case may be
received his representation; or
iii.the complainant is not satisfied with the reply given to him by the
insurer or insurance broker, as the case may be;
b)The complaint is made within one year—
i.after the order of the insurer rejecting the representation is received;
or
ii.after receipt of decision of the insurer or insurance broker, as the", "metadata": {"source_file": "4ec93fc12bff.pdf", "page_number": 5, "chunk_index": 45, "page_chunk_index": 0}, "score": 0.1116}, {"text": "located.
2.The complaint shall be in writing, duly signed or made by way of
electronic  mail  or  online  through  the  website  of  the  Council  for
Insurance Ombudsmen by the complainant or through his legal heirs,
Nominee or Assignee and shall state clearly the name and address of
the complainant, the name of the branch or office of the insurer
against whom the complaint is made, the facts giving rise to the", "metadata": {"source_file": "4ec93fc12bff.pdf", "page_number": 4, "chunk_index": 44, "page_chunk_index": 10}, "score": 0.1095}, {"text": "Shikha”, 60-B, Hoshangabad Road, Opp. Gayatri Mandir, Bhopal –
462011.  Tel:  0755-2769201,  2769202  and  2769203  Email:
bimalokpal.bhopal@cioins.co.in
